source: Autonomous web research by Chief Decifer
synthesis: The strongest consensus across 2024-2025 research is that plain Kelly criterion is dangerous for live trading systems due to drawdown sensitivity and estimation error amplification — fractional Kelly (0.25–0.5x) with an explicit drawdown probability constraint (Busseti et al.'s risk-constrained Kelly) is the modern institutional baseline. Volatility-targeting — sizing positions so that each trade contributes a fixed annualised volatility budget (formula: w_t = σ* / (√252 · σ_t)) using GARCH or ATR — consistently outperforms static sizing across regimes and is the single highest-leverage improvement available for a system like Decifer. Portfolio-level 'heat' management (tracking correlation-adjusted total risk across all open positions) is widely identified as the hidden risk that kills retail and early-stage algo accounts, because correlated positions amplify effective exposure well beyond per-trade limits. Cross-asset correlation is increasingly unreliable as a diversification tool — BlackRock (2025) and PGIM (2024) both note the breakdown of stock-bond negative correlation, making rolling correlation monitoring and sector concentration caps essential for multi-user risk profiles. CVaR (Conditional Value at Risk / Expected Shortfall) is theoretically superior to VaR for tail-risk management because it quantifies expected loss beyond the threshold, not just the threshold itself, making it the right metric for Decifer's per-account risk budget display and circuit-breaker logic.
top_3_quick_wins: VIX-Adjusted Fractional Kelly Sizer — replace raw Kelly with a volatility-scaled fractional Kelly that reduces position size when VIX-rank is elevated, directly upgrading the existing Kelly implementation with 1 formula change

Portfolio Heat Monitor with Correlation-Adjusted Exposure Cap — compute total correlation-weighted risk across open positions before every new order and block/reduce trades that would breach the heat limit

ATR-Based Volatility-Targeting Layer — wrap all position size calculations in a σ*/σ_t multiplier using 14-day ATR so every trade targets the same annualised risk budget regardless of instrument or regime

findings.expected_impact: Reduces maximum drawdown 20-35% vs full Kelly while retaining 51-75% of growth; backtested Kelly strategies with VIX-rank scaling returned 2.53%-17.24% annualised with volatility well below buy-and-hold benchmark
findings.summary: Replace Decifer's current static Kelly fraction with a dynamic fraction that scales inversely with VIX-rank (a normalised percentile of the VIX over a trailing 126-252 day window). During low-VIX periods the system bets closer to half-Kelly; during high-VIX spikes it automatically drops to quarter-Kelly or lower. This directly addresses the core weakness of the current implementation — Kelly is theoretically optimal only under known, stable probabilities, and Decifer's 9-dimension signal scores are noisy estimates that will systematically overstate edge during volatile regimes.
findings.upgrades: Upgrades the existing Kelly criterion position sizer with a volatility-regime multiplier drawn from freely available VIX data via yfinance
findings.what_changes: Add a vix_rank() helper that fetches ^VIX history via yfinance, computes the rolling percentile over 126 days, and returns a scalar in [0,1]. Multiply the raw Kelly fraction by (1 - vix_rank * 0.5) so that at VIX-rank=0 the full fraction is used and at VIX-rank=1 the fraction is halved. Apply a hard cap of 0.25 (quarter-Kelly maximum) as a safety floor. Expose kelly_multiplier as a per-account config parameter so future multi-user profiles can set their own risk appetite.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer is in paper trading with an unproven edge. Busseti et al. (2016) and the arxiv paper 'Sizing the Risk: Kelly, VIX, and Hybrid Approaches' (2024) both show that fractional Kelly strategies reduce volatility more than proportionally to the growth they sacrifice — betting 50% of Kelly returns 75% of optimal growth but with only 25% of the variance. For a system transitioning to live money held by friends and family, surviving drawdowns is more important than maximising growth rate.
findings.source_evidence: Wysocki (2024), 'Sizing the Risk: Kelly, VIX, and Hybrid Approaches', arxiv.org/pdf/2508.16598; Busseti, Ryu, Boyd (2016) 'Risk-Constrained Kelly Gambling', Journal of Investing 25(3); QuantInsti blog 'The Risk-Constrained Kelly Criterion' (July 2025), blog.quantinsti.com/risk-constrained-kelly-criterion/

findings.expected_impact: Targets constant annualised risk per trade (e.g. 1% NAV) regardless of instrument or regime; Moreira & Muir (2017) framework implemented in live backtests (2015-2025) shows significantly improved Sharpe and information ratios vs static sizing across all asset classes
findings.summary: Implement the volatility-targeting formula w_t = σ* / (√252 · σ_t) where σ* is a target annualised volatility per trade (e.g. 1.5%) and σ_t is the 14-day ATR expressed as a percentage of current price. This automatically shrinks position size when a stock is moving fast and grows it when volatility is compressed — the opposite of what emotional traders do. It is the most empirically validated position sizing technique in recent quantitative literature and requires only ATR, which yfinance provides.
findings.upgrades: Adds a volatility-targeting wrapper around the existing Kelly sizer so both methods work together: Kelly sets the signal-confidence-based size ceiling, ATR-targeting sets the volatility-normalised size floor/target
findings.what_changes: Add compute_atr_vol_target(ticker, target_ann_vol=0.15, atr_period=14) that fetches OHLCV from yfinance, computes ATR, converts to % of price, annualises by √252, then returns position_size = (account_equity * target_ann_vol) / (annualised_atr * price). Blend with Kelly output: final_size = min(kelly_size, atr_vol_target_size). Add target_annualised_vol as a per-account config field so conservative user profiles can set 0.08 and aggressive profiles 0.20.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer currently runs with a bullish bias and its 9-dimension signals will fire on high-momentum stocks that are also high-volatility. Without ATR-based sizing, the system will systematically over-size into the most volatile trades at exactly the wrong moment. The mql5 practitioner blog (March 2026) shows this exact formula achieving consistent performance improvement across eight asset classes in out-of-sample testing from 2015-2025.
findings.source_evidence: Moreira & Muir (2017) 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios'; MQL5 practitioner blog 'Time Series Econometrics and GARCH Volatility Models in Algorithmic Trading Part 2' (March 2026), mql5.com/en/blogs/post/768148; QuantPedia 'An Introduction to Volatility Targeting', quantpedia.com/an-introduction-to-volatility-targeting/

findings.feature: Portfolio Heat Monitor with Correlation-Adjusted Exposure Cap
findings.expected_impact: Prevents catastrophic correlated drawdowns; Signal Pilot case study shows 6 positions × 1% risk with 0.85 average correlation creates 13.6% actual effective risk — the formula Effective Risk = Nominal Risk × √(N × Avg Correlation) corrects for this systematically
findings.summary: Before any new order is submitted, compute the portfolio's total correlation-adjusted heat: sum each open position's dollar-risk, then scale by √(N × avg_pairwise_correlation) to get effective heat as a % of NAV. Block new orders that would push effective heat above a configurable threshold (default 5% for conservative, 8% for aggressive profiles). This is the single most-cited failure mode in retail and early-stage algo trading — positions that look diversified by ticker are often highly correlated and create cluster risk during sector events.
findings.upgrades: Adds a pre-trade portfolio heat gate to the order routing pipeline, blocking or downsizing orders that would breach heat limits — directly enabling the planned Per-Account Risk Limits backlog item
findings.what_changes: Add compute_portfolio_heat(open_positions, proposed_trade, lookback=30) that: (1) fetches 30-day returns for all open position tickers plus proposed ticker via yfinance, (2) computes average pairwise Pearson correlation, (3) calculates effective heat using the correlation-adjusted formula, (4) compares to per-account max_heat threshold from config. If breach detected, either reject trade or scale size down proportionally. Emit a heat_warning event to the dashboard. Expose max_portfolio_heat as a per-account setting.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer targets friends and family with diverse risk tolerances. As the system scales to multiple accounts, a concentrated sector event (like the July 2024 tech drawdown where the S&P tech sector fell 15% vs 7% for the index) could hit all accounts simultaneously. Correlation-adjusted heat caps are how institutional desks prevent this — and ChartMini, SwingFolio, and Pro Trader Dashboard all now offer this as a core feature, signalling that it is table-stakes for any trading platform.
findings.source_evidence: Signal Pilot Advanced Risk Management curriculum (education.signalpilot.io); ChartMini 'Portfolio Heat Management: The Hidden Risk That Destroys Traders' (Jan 2026); International Trading Institute 'Dynamic Position Sizing and Risk Management in Volatile Markets' (Oct 2025), internationaltradinginstitute.com

findings.feature: Drawdown Brake — Tiered Equity Curve Circuit Breaker
findings.expected_impact: Institutional three-tier drawdown protocol reduces risk-per-trade 25% at -5% drawdown, 50% at -10%, and halts at -15%; Busseti risk-constrained Kelly shows smoother equity curves with constraint Prob(wealth < alpha) < beta
findings.summary: Implement a three-tier drawdown brake that automatically reduces Kelly/ATR sizing multipliers as the account drawdown from its rolling peak deepens. Tier 1 (-5% from peak): reduce all position sizes by 25%. Tier 2 (-10% to -15%): reduce by 50% and restrict to highest-conviction signals only (score > 7.5). Tier 3 (> -15%): halt new trades for a configurable cooldown period and alert user via Telegram. This is a direct implementation of the risk-constrained Kelly constraint and the institutional drawdown protocol documented in practitioner literature.
findings.upgrades: Upgrades the existing Kelly criterion sizer with a drawdown-aware multiplier that scales position sizes down as accounts move into drawdown, and integrates with the planned Per-Account Risk Limits backlog item
findings.what_changes: Add compute_drawdown_brake(account_id) that retrieves peak NAV from trade journal, computes current drawdown%, and returns a sizing_multiplier in [0, 1]. Apply multiplier to all outgoing position sizes before order submission. Add drawdown_tier_1_threshold, drawdown_tier_2_threshold, drawdown_halt_threshold as per-account config fields with safe defaults (0.05, 0.10, 0.15). Log brake activation to the unified trade journal and push Telegram notification on tier change.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer is paper trading now but will transition to live money. A string of losses on a young, unproven signal system is statistically inevitable. Without a drawdown brake, the system will continue sizing normally through a losing streak, compounding losses. The risk-constrained Kelly literature (Busseti 2016, QuantInsti 2024) shows that adding Prob(minimum wealth < alpha) < beta as a constraint produces significantly smoother equity curves — the drawdown brake is a practical discrete implementation of this constraint.
findings.source_evidence: International Trading Institute 'Dynamic Position Sizing and Risk Management in Volatile Markets' (Oct 2025); Busseti, Ryu, Boyd (2016) 'Risk-Constrained Kelly Gambling'; QuantInsti 'The Risk-Constrained Kelly Criterion' (July 2025); Medium 'Position Sizing Strategies for Algo-Traders' (Aug 2024), medium.com/@jpolec_72972

findings.feature: Rolling CVaR Dashboard Metric and Per-Account Risk Budget
findings.expected_impact: CVaR at 95% confidence captures 10%+ more tail risk than VaR in non-normal return distributions; replaces VaR as the primary risk display metric for users who set conservative profiles
findings.summary: Compute a rolling 30-day CVaR (Conditional Value at Risk, also called Expected Shortfall) for each account's position portfolio and display it on the per-account dashboard. CVaR tells users not just 'your worst 5% of days will be at least -X%' (VaR) but 'when you are in that worst 5%, you should expect to lose -Y%' — a meaningfully more informative metric for tail risk. Research consistently shows CVaR is a coherent risk measure that CVaR minimisation also minimises VaR, making it strictly superior for risk-budget display.
findings.upgrades: Adds a CVaR-based risk budget display to the per-account dashboard, complementing (not replacing) the existing P&L view and enabling the planned Per-Account Local Dashboard backlog item with institutional-grade risk metrics
findings.what_changes: Add compute_portfolio_cvar(account_id, confidence=0.95, lookback_days=30) that: (1) retrieves last 30 days of daily P&L from trade journal, (2) sorts returns ascending, (3) takes the mean of the bottom 5% as CVaR, (4) expresses as % of account NAV. Display on dashboard as 'Tail Risk (CVaR 95%): -X.X%'. Add a per-account cvar_budget config field; if daily CVaR exceeds budget, emit a warning and optionally reduce the sizing multiplier. Use historical simulation method (no scipy/statsmodels needed — pure numpy).
findings.why_it_matters: As Decifer moves toward a multi-user SaaS with friends and family capital, users will need to understand and communicate their real downside risk. VaR is legally and conceptually inadequate — it tells you the minimum loss in the worst cases, not the expected magnitude. For a conservative user profile, CVaR at 95% expressed as a daily dollar figure is the most honest and actionable risk metric available. The IAENG 2025 paper on CVaR-based portfolios with LLM views specifically finds Claude-based CVaR portfolios achieve high Sharpe ratios.
findings.source_evidence: Örebro University Working Paper 2024, 'Minimum VaR and Minimum CVaR Optimal Portfolios' (oru.se/globalassets); IAENG 2025 'CVaR-based Portfolio with Investment Views Given by Large Language Models' (iaeng.org/IJAM/issues_v55); Rockafellar & Uryasev 'Optimization of CVaR', Journal of Risk 2000 (canonical reference)

findings.feature: Intra-Portfolio Rolling Correlation Guard with Sector Cap
findings.expected_impact: Prevents hidden correlation clustering; during the 10% worst equity months since 1970, stock-bond correlation shifts from -0.20 to +0.15, eliminating 60% of expected diversification — correlation guard catches this before it hits the portfolio
findings.summary: Compute rolling 30-day pairwise correlations between all open positions and flag when any two positions exceed 0.7 correlation, or when the portfolio's average pairwise correlation exceeds 0.5. Additionally, cap total risk exposure in any single GICS sector to 30% of portfolio heat. This upgrades the already-planned Cross-Account Correlation Guard backlog item with concrete formulas and thresholds. BlackRock (2025) and PGIM (2024) both cite shifting correlations as the central risk in modern portfolio construction.
findings.upgrades: Implements the planned Cross-Account Correlation Guard backlog item with concrete correlation thresholds, sector caps, and free-data implementation via yfinance
findings.what_changes: Add correlation_guard(open_positions, proposed_ticker, lookback=30) that: (1) fetches 30-day daily returns for all tickers via yfinance, (2) computes full pairwise correlation matrix, (3) checks if proposed_ticker has correlation > 0.70 with any existing position, (4) checks if proposed sector (from yfinance .info['sector']) would breach 30% sector heat cap, (5) returns (ALLOW, WARN, BLOCK) enum with reason. Add correlation heatmap data to dashboard WebSocket broadcaster payload. Log all BLOCK decisions to unified trade journal with correlation data.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer's current signal system is bullish-biased and focuses on trend/momentum/breakout signals that will systematically select correlated winners during bull runs. When the trend reverses, correlated positions will all hit stops simultaneously. HL Hunt Financial (March 2025) documents that institutions track 60/120/252-day rolling correlations and flag when deviations exceed 0.3 from long-term averages — Decifer needs at minimum a 30-day version of this to avoid concentration blowups as it scales.
findings.source_evidence: HL Hunt Financial 'Cross-Asset Correlation Dynamics and Regime Shift Analysis' (March 2025), hlhunt.org; BlackRock 'Investment Directions Fall 2025' (blackrock.com); PGIM Quant 'Cross-Asset Correlations in Market Turbulence' (2024, pgim.com)

findings.feature: Regime-Conditioned Position Size Scalar (VIX Regime + HMM Integration)
findings.expected_impact: Volatility-managed portfolios (Moreira & Muir 2017) reduce drawdowns in recessions and improve Sharpe; GARCH-regime system backtested 2015-2025 shows consistent improvement vs buy-and-hold across 8 asset classes; PortRSMs (MDPI 2025) achieves +0.03 Sharpe improvement in US markets
findings.summary: Connect the already-planned HMM Regime Detection backlog item to position sizing: when HMM detects a high-volatility or bear regime, apply an additional 0.5x multiplier to all position sizes. When HMM detects a low-volatility bull regime, allow sizes up to but not exceeding the ATR volatility-target level. This creates a two-layer regime-aware sizing system: ATR handles instrument-level volatility, HMM handles portfolio-level regime risk. Research from PHBS Working Paper (2024) and the MQL5 GARCH system (2026) both confirm this layered approach outperforms single-method sizing.
findings.upgrades: Bridges the planned HMM Regime Detection backlog item to the risk management layer, creating the first regime-aware position sizing in Decifer
findings.what_changes: Add get_regime_scalar(regime_state) that maps HMM regime output (bull/bear/neutral) to a sizing scalar: bull=1.0, neutral=0.75, bear=0.5, crash=0.25. Multiply all final position sizes by this scalar. If HMM is not yet implemented, fall back to a simple VIX-level regime: VIX < 15 → 1.0, VIX 15-25 → 0.75, VIX 25-35 → 0.5, VIX > 35 → 0.25. Store regime_state and applied_scalar in the trade journal for each order for later walk-forward calibration.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer's HMM Regime Detection is already planned but currently disconnected from risk management. The PHBS Working Paper (2024) by Yang on volatility-managed trading shows that reducing position weights after volatility spikes (which accompany regime transitions) is the primary driver of drawdown reduction in volatility-managed portfolios. Wiring regime state into sizing is the natural second step after regime detection is built.
findings.source_evidence: Yang (2024) 'Volatility-Managed Volatility Trading', PHBS Working Paper, english.phbs.pku.edu.cn; MQL5 'Time Series Econometrics and GARCH Volatility Models Part 2' (March 2026); Liu & Ichise (2025) 'PortRSMs: Learning Regime Shifts for Portfolio Policy', MDPI JRFM 18(8):434

findings.feature: Per-Account Risk Profile Config Schema with Multi-Tier Presets
findings.expected_impact: Enables safe onboarding of friends and family with different risk tolerances; prevents the most common multi-user SaaS failure mode where a single risk config is applied to all accounts regardless of individual circumstances
findings.summary: Define a structured RiskProfile dataclass that encodes all risk parameters in one place: kelly_fraction (0.25 default), target_ann_vol (0.12), max_portfolio_heat (0.05), max_sector_concentration (0.30), max_single_position_pct (0.10), drawdown_tier1/2/halt thresholds, cvar_budget, and correlation_block_threshold. Ship three preset profiles: Conservative (friends/family, low risk tolerance), Moderate (default), and Aggressive (founder account). This is the foundational config layer that makes all the other risk features per-user-configurable — directly enabling the Per-Account Config Profiles and Per-Account Risk Limits backlog items.
findings.upgrades: Provides the configuration backbone that all other risk features in this research report depend on, enabling the Per-Account Config Profiles and Per-Account Risk Limits backlog items
findings.what_changes: Create a RiskProfile dataclass in config.py with all fields listed above and sensible defaults. Update risk.py to accept a RiskProfile object rather than hardcoded constants. Add RISK_PROFILES dict with Conservative/Moderate/Aggressive presets. Update the AccountDecisionAgent to load the appropriate RiskProfile per account_id from the per-account config. Add a validate_risk_profile() function that checks for logical consistency (e.g. halt threshold must be > tier2 threshold). Expose profile selection in the one-time user setup screen.
findings.why_it_matters: Research across all sources — from fractional Kelly literature to institutional portfolio heat management — consistently shows that risk parameters must be calibrated to individual investor circumstances. For a multi-user SaaS serving friends and family, a 60-year-old conservative investor and a 30-year-old founder cannot share the same Kelly fraction, heat limit, or CVaR budget. The One-Time User Setup Screen and Per-User Dashboard Authentication backlog items both depend on this config layer existing.
findings.source_evidence: nickyoder.com 'The Kelly Criterion' (Dec 2024) on professional investor risk levels; ChartMini 'Portfolio Heat Management' (Jan 2026); Signal Pilot 'Advanced Risk Management' curriculum — all sources converge on parameterised, per-user risk management as the institutional standard
